coredata.dc:title: In vivo fluorescence non-enzymatic glucose sensing technique for diabetes management by CQDs incorporated dextran nanocomposites in human blood serums
coredata.dc:creator: Patra, Swapnita
Purohit, Shuvendu Shuvankar
Swain, Sarat K.
coredata.dc:description: Herein, carbon quantum dot (CQD) doped dextran/polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) (CQD/Dex/PVA) nanocomposites are synthesized for in vivo detection of glucose by non-enzymatic “switch-OFF” fluorescence mechanism in real human blood serums. Highly functional, dispersible and stable CQD is obtained from aloe vera by carbonization and a facile solution mixing technique is adopted for the preparation of CQD/Dex/PVA nanocomposites. It is noticed that hydrogen bonding between –OH group of glucose and that of the dextran and PVA quenches the fluorescence intensity of the nanocomposite. The synthesized CQD/Dex/PVA nanocomposite is highly selective for sensing of glucose in presence of other interfering sugars which proves its suitability to be implemented in complex biological environment. This biosensing technique exhibits linear range from 0.27 mM to 1.72 mM, R2 = 0.986 and a low detection limit of 61.1 μM. The measurements of glucose content in twelve real human blood serums are done and compared with commercial glucometer with satisfactory recoveries. Considering the high selectivity, sensitivity and accuracy, this “switch-OFF” fluorescence method holds promising application in diabetes management.

head.ce:title: In vivo fluorescence non-enzymatic glucose sensing technique for diabetes management by CQDs incorporated dextran nanocomposites in human blood serums

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Veer Surendra Sai University of Technology, Burla, Sambalpur 768018, Odisha, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Veer Surendra Sai University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Veer Surendra Sai University of Technology, Burla, Sambalpur 768018, Odisha, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, carbon quantum dot (CQD) doped dextran/polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) (CQD/Dex/PVA) nanocomposites are synthesized for in vivo detection of glucose by non-enzymatic “switch-OFF” fluorescence mechanism in real human blood serums. Highly functional, dispersible and stable CQD is obtained from aloe vera by carbonization and a facile solution mixing technique is adopted for the preparation of CQD/Dex/PVA nanocomposites. It is noticed that hydrogen bonding between –OH group of glucose and that of the dextran and PVA quenches the fluorescence intensity of the nanocomposite. The synthesized CQD/Dex/PVA nanocomposite is highly selective for sensing of glucose in presence of other interfering sugars which proves its suitability to be implemented in complex biological environment. This biosensing technique exhibits linear range from 0.27 mM to 1.72 mM, R2 = 0.986 and a low detection limit of 61.1 μM. The measurements of glucose content in twelve real human blood serums are done and compared with commercial glucometer with satisfactory recoveries. Considering the high selectivity, sensitivity and accuracy, this “switch-OFF” fluorescence method holds promising application in diabetes management.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Persistent checking and maintaining blood glucose level within a suitable target range is essential for diabetes patients to prevent long-term grievous health complications such as, heart attack, blindness, kidney disease and amputation [1]. Accordingly, this crucial necessity of glucose sensing devices is dramatically extending in consequence of the increasing prevalence of diabetes mellitus [2,3]. The conventional biological sensors have various limitations including low sensitivity and unsatisfactory selectivity. However, the performance of nanotechnology by means of nanocomposite sensors remarkably prevails over those limitations by standing as a potential candidate for glucose sensing owing to their significantly high surface area, improved catalytic properties, sensitivity, selectivity and specificity [4–7]. For the designing of an efficient, highly sensitive and selective nanocomposite sensor, the incorporation of appropriate sensing material is vital [8]. For this purpose, we have prepared CQDs doped dextran assisted PVA nanocomposite for sensing of glucose.

Fluorescent sensorspermit change in signal in response to a particular analyte in association with a fluorescent sensing molecule [9]. Recently, fluorescence mechanism is intensively implemented for glucose sensing in view of many important advantages such as excellent sensitivity, selectivity, specificity, accuracy, portability, ease of operation, miniaturization and instantaneous responsein comparison to other sensing techniques [10,11]. Moreover, this non-invasive and minimally-invasive glucose monitoring technique can uproot the drawbacks of current invasive, expensive, painful and cumbersome glucose monitoring methods [12]. Fluorescence “turn ON-OFF” [13], “turn OFF-ON” [14], enhancement [15,16] and quenching [17,18] are the different types of fluorescence sensing mechanisms adopted for glucose sensing.

As a better replacement of conventional fluorescence sensing probes, CQDs have drawn keen interest on account of their low cost, nontoxicity, environmental friendliness, chemical stability, excellent optical properties, facile preparation routes and good dispersibility [19]. CQDs consist of discrete, quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles of below 10 nm size and first accidentally discovered in 2004 during purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes [20]. The advantage of choosing CQDs over other fluorescent probes is the ease of surface passivation and functionalization through which the optical properties can be properly tuned [21]. Additionally, the fluorescence property of CQDs effectively responds to polarity [22], pH [23] and ionic strength [24,25] of solution which again extends their potentiality for sensing applications. All these exceptional properties of CQDs made them highly suitable for application in numerous fields like chemical and biological sensing [26–28], heavy metal sensing [29], drug delivery [30], bioimaging [31], solar cells [32], electrocatalysis [33] and photocatalysis [34].

Dextranis a biodegradable heterogeneouspolysaccharidecomprises of repeating glucose monomers and synthesized from sucrose by bacterium Leuconostoc mesenteroides [35]. The main chain of dextran polymer composed of α-1,6glycosidiclinkages connecting glucose monomers, and the branches are linked with α-1,3 linkages [36]. Dextran exhibits several physical and chemical advantages like superior surface resistance, water solubility and simple chemical derivatization which helps in its modification through blending with other fluorescence sensing molecules for the preparation of a finer immobilization agent to be used in biosensors [37,38]. This modification and further integration of dextran promisingly contributes to the enhancement of fluorescence sensing performance. The above mentioned properties of dextran prove its extensive application in sensing among other polysaccharides. Dextran has also noteworthy applications in pharmaceutical and chemical industries as stabilizer [39], emulsifier [40] and carrier [41]. PVA is popular as a synthetic,biocompatible, biodegradable and water soluble polymerwhich is obtained by the alkaline hydrolysis of polyvinyl acetate and it also possesses excellent mechanical properties [42]. PVA is frequently modified with nanoparticles and other materials for the fabrication of nanocomposite sensors due to its outstanding film-forming ability, high tensile strength, flexibility, excellent adhesive and emulsifying properties suitable for immense biosensing applications [43]. Utilization of PVA as matrix in nanocomposites provides well stabilized structures and inhibits agglomeration of nanoparticles [44,45].

Although numerous numbers of articles are available on the preparation of materials for glucose sensing however, dextran based CQD doped material is new. The main reason behind using dextran is its property showing enhancement in fluorescence intensity with CQD. The excellent biocompatibility of dextran polysaccharide makes it convenient to work in the complex biological environment and suitable for blood glucose detection. PVA is utilized with dextran as it provides stability and strength to the matrix by binding with dextran chain. So, Dex/PVA together makes a highly biocompatible and stable composite matrix for blood glucose sensing with high sensitivity. Compared to the reported glucose sensing techniques, the presented non-enzymatic “switch-OFF” fluorescence sensing mechanism by CQD/Dex/PVA nanocomposite has many advantageous features. First of all, the preparation of functionalized CQD with good dispersibility and colloidal stability is one of the key results of the present study. Then the change in fluorescence intensities of dextran, PVA, CQD and their combinations supporting “switch-OFF” mechanism towards high accuracy sensing are studied. Again, the adoption of non-enzymatic method has additive advantages like favourable stability, easy preparation process and good economic value due to exclusion of expensive enzymes. Selectivity test with various interfering sugars proved the excellent selectivity and suitability of this method for sensing in complex biological system. The main focus of the work is to measure glucose content in real human blood serums. So, the process is applied to detect glucose concentration in twelve human blood serums and the results are compared with commercial glucometer for better reliability. Moreover, the excellent stimuli responsiveness of the as-synthesized material in accordance with the blood pH and stability in different ionic environment are also studied.

Dextran (Mw = 10,000) and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) (Mw = 60,000) having degree of hydrolysis 86.5–89.0 mol% is purchased from Hi-Media chemicals Pvt. Ltd., Mumbai, India. Glucose and fructose are obtained from CDH, Central drug house (P) Ltd., New Delhi, India. Other saccharides such as galactose, mannose, arabinose, and xylose are procured from Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd., Mumbai, India. All reagents are of analytical grade and are used as received without any further purification. Human blood specimens are collected from the pathology lab of a nearby hospital without any pretreatment. All solutions used in experiment are prepared with double distilled water.

CQD is synthesized from aloe vera leaf gel by carbonization using earlier reported method [30]. Fresh and green leaves of aloe vera are first cleaned with double distilled water to eliminate dust particles and extra adherent contaminations. The surface skin of aloe vera leaf is then detached with a fine bladed knife. The leaf is then cut up into slices and washed with double distilled water, followed by drying in open air. The aloe vera leaf pieces are then taken in a ceramic crucible and kept in a tube furnace for carbonization at a temperature of 250 °C and heating rate of 10 °C per minute. After 2 h, the dark black outcome is left to settle at ambient temperature and then mechanically crushed into fine granules. The carbonized sample of 0.2 g is then put into 50 mL distilled water and ultrasonically disseminated for 30 min at 120 kHz/60 W. The resulted black solution is centrifuged and then filtered with 0.22 µm membrane for the removal of big particles. The supernatant CQDs solution is then stored at 4 °C for characterization. The complete synthesis procedure of water soluble CQDs is schematically represented in the Scheme S1a.

Synthesis procedure of CQD/Dex/PVA nanocomposite is given in the Scheme S1b. First 1 wt% stock solutions of dextran and PVA are prepared with distilled water. Then a specific quantity of CQDs aqueous solution is added to dextran followed by stirring for 1 h. Then PVA is added to the CQD/Dex mixture proceeding to again stirring for 1 h. Then the solution mixture is dried in an oven at 60 °C to form CQD/Dex/PVA nanocomposite.

The UV–Vis absorbance is determined using Shimadzu UV‐2550 UV-Vis spectrophotometer. The fluorescence emission data are collected with a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent technologies). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of pure CQD and CQD/PVA, CQD/Dex/PVA, Dex/PVA and CQD/Dex/PVA@Glu composites are recorded using a Perkin-Elmer FTIR spectrometer, USA, operating at a frequency range of 4000 to 400 cm−1. The zeta potential measurements are carried out by a Zetasizer Nano Instrument (Malvern) with model number: ZEN-3690. The surface morphologies of CQD/Dex/PVA and CQD/Dex/PVA@Glu nanocomposites are characterized by field emission scanningelectron microscope(FESEM) from Jeol Ltd., Japan (JSM-6700F).

At first the prepared CQDs aqueous solution is mixed with the stock solution of dextran of a certain concentration in 1:1 ratio at room temperature. Then to this solution of CQD and dextran, PVA is added. After the required incubation time, the mixture solution of CQD, dextran and PVA is taken in a quartz cuvette for fluorescence test at 330 nm. The enhancement of fluorescence emission intensity than pure CQD is observed. Then glucose is added to the reaction mixture and incubated for 15 mins at room temperature. The addition of glucose quenched the fluorescence emission intensity gradually returning back from enhanced state even too much lower than the intensity of CQD. Then the fluorescence intensity is tested by taking different concentrations of glucose up to 100 mg/mL and subsequent decrease in intensity is observed with increasing the concentration of glucose. This decrease in fluorescence intensity with increase in glucose concentration occurs according to the “switch-OFF” mechanism of glucose sensing. CQDs when incorporated into dextran, the fluorescence intensity of CQD/Dex composite increases remarkably. Similar observation is noticed in the previous report where dextran coated semiconductor quantum dots showed high luminescence property [46]. When PVA is added to CQD/Dex composite, PVA binds with dextran chain participating in the formation of matrix of composite. However, in this stage the fluorescence intensity decreases slightly due to entanglement of PVA and dextran polymer chains. But this small loss in fluorescence intensity by addition of PVA can be sacrificed as PVA provides stability to the composite matrix. Finally, when glucose added to CQD/Dex/PVA composite the fluorescence intensity decreases which is ascribed to the strong interaction of hydroxyl groups of glucose with the hydroxyl groups present in the CQD/Dex/PVA composite. This “switch-OFF” technique for fluorescence sensing of glucose is schematically represented in the Scheme 1 a. The picture of composites under UV lamp is given in the Scheme 1b and the interactions of CQD, dextran, PVA and their composites with glucose are schematically represented in the Scheme 1c. Then the selectivity test of as-synthesized nanocomposite towards glucose detection is carried out by taking the fluorescence emission intensity of nanocomposite by adding other saccharides such as fructose, galactose, mannose, arabinose and xylose in place of glucose keeping their concentrations same to glucose solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring glucose in real human blood serums
ce:section.ce:para: Twelve blood specimens of both diabetic and normal people are collected from the pathology lab of nearby hospital. The blood samples are subjected to ultracentrifugation at 10000 rpm for 30 mins to collect the blood serums as centrifugal supernatant. Then the diluted form of these serum samples is added to the reaction system and taken for fluorescence study.

The presence of isolated π-bonding network of sp2 carbon backbones is primarily responsible for the photophysical properties of CQDs [47]. Fig. S1 a shows the UV–Vis spectra of the prepared CQDs. It is observed that, there is an absorption peak at wavelength of 300 nm which corresponds to π-π* transition of C = C bonds of carbon network, indicating the possibility for formation of CQDs. The spectrum in this range is mostly reported for bare CQDs irrespective of the preparation method [48–50]. Besides this one UV peak, the long tail extending towards visible range attributes to the nanoparticle functionalization resulting low energy surface centres. Under UV lamp the CQD aqueous solution showed blue fluorescence at 365 nm, whereas, at 254 nm no fluorescence is observed which is shown in inset of Fig. S1a. To investigate the excitation dependent emission spectra of the synthesized CQDs the fluorescence emission spectra is recorded at different excitation wavelengths from 278 nm to 470 nm as shown in the Fig. S1b. It is noticed that the fluorescence emission shows peaks at different intensities with change in excitation wavelength, giving the appearance of a net like arrangement of plots. This excitation dependent fluorescence emission nature of CQDs is a photophysical characteristic mainly originates from surface states or defects present in these nanomaterials. The clearly noticeable redshift of emission wavelengths depending on the subsequent increase in excitation wavelength indicates the tunable emissive behaviour of CQDs depicting the multicolour nature of CQDs. Moreover, it is observed that the peak width goes on increasing with increase in excitation wavelength. This broadening of emission peaks is ascribed to the presence of multiple emitting species in the CQDs. Again, the decrease in intensity of emission peaks with increase in excitation wavelength proves the presence of π-π* transition as mentioned in UV–Vis analysis. This results clearly evidenced the formation and intense photophysical properties of the as synthesized CQDs in accordance with the previous literatures [51–54].

Surface functionalization of CQD is characterized in FTIR spectra (Fig. S1c). Two prominent transmittance peaks at 3316 cm−1 and 1640 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching frequency of O-H and > C = O of carboxyl group respectively. These peaks not only give the affirmation of hydroxyl group and carboxylic group on the surface of CQD but also, it is cleared the absence of any inorganic moieties. Similar results are obtained in the literature during study of the surface functionality of CQD [55,56]. The functionalized CQD is a potential candidate to form composite with polar functionalized polymer network of PVA and dextran. In order to know the chemical interaction and surface characteristics of CQD/PVA, CQD/Dex/PVA, Dex/PVA and CQD/Dex/PVA@Glu, the FTIR spectra are comparatively established in the Fig. S1d. In FTIR spectra of CQD/PVA a sharp peak at 3285 cm−1 corresponds to O-H stretching frequency. This shifting of transmittance peak of O-H stretching is due to the intermolecular hydrogen bonding of hydroxyl group of PVA with the surface of CQD. Similar observation is also noticed in case of CQD/Dex/PVA, Dex/PVA and CQD/Dex/PVA@Glu. In the FTIR spectra of CQD/PVA, a O-H bending peak appears at 1330 cm−1 while in case of Dex/PVA the intensity of this peak decreased which is due to the interaction between the hydroxyl groups of dextran and PVA. The addition of CQD to Dex/PVA nanocomposite imparts a peak at 1760 cm−1 in the FTIR spectra of CQD/Dex/PVA which is attributed to the > C = O stretching vibration of the carboxyl group present in the surface of CQD. It is interesting to found that, the O-H stretching peak becomes broader in case of CQD/Dex/PVA@Glu indicating the absence of free hydroxyl group. This is an evidence to establish the mechanism of interaction of glucose with CQD, PVA, dextran and their composites. From this observation, it is assumed that the free hydroxyl groups are strongly interacting with the hydroxyl groups of glucose. Again, after the addition of glucose to the CQD/Dex/PVA nanocomposite a transmittance peak appears at 1720 cm−1 due to the > C = O stretching of aldehyde group. The presence of C-OH bonds is evidenced from the appearance of strong peak between 1050 cm−1 to 1100 cm−1 in all plots. The C-O stretching peak at 1050 cm−1 and 1100 cm−1 are in accordance with the presence of primary and secondary hydroxyl groups respectively. The presence of strong peak at 1050 cm−1 in the FTIR plot of CQD/Dex/PVA@Glu indicates the presence of primary hydroxyl group of glucose. The transmittance peak at 1692 cm−1 in FTIR spectra of CQD/PVA, CQD/Dex/PVA and CQD/Dex/PVA@Glu is due to the presence of carboxyl group at the surface of CQD. Furthermore, a C-O-C stretching vibration at 1034 cm−1 is observed which is due to pyranose ring vibration of glucose and dextran. Different vibrational peaks at respective wave number of FTIR spectra of CQD, CQD/PVA, CQD/Dex/PVA, Dex/PVA and CQD/Dex/PVA@Glu are comparatively summarized as Table S1.

Colloidal stabilities of CQD and its composites are investigated by zeta potential plot in the Fig. S2. It is found that CQD gives the peak at −28.2 mV with a zeta deviation of 13.3 mV (Fig. S2a). The negative value of zeta potential evidences the possible attachment of negatively charged functional groups like OH–, COO–, which has been verified in FTIR study in the Fig. S1c. The presence of negatively charged moieties gives an excellent dispersion of CQD in aqueous based solvent. Further, surface negative charge in zeta potential curve supports the colloidal stability of CQD [56,57]. To prove this statement, we have stored the CQD at room temperature for eight months and we did not get any turbidity in the CQD. Hence, the colloidal stability of prepared CQD is sufficiently high which can be used over a period of time. The zeta potential of CQD/Dex is recorded in the Fig. S2b. It is found that there are two peaks are obtained at −34.2 mV and 28.9 mV. In this plot the surface negative charged zeta potential is appeared along with a positive charged peak. This is due to excellent steric stabilization and high molecular weight of non-ionic dextran polymer. It works as a bridge and show patches of positive areas along with usual negative potential. Again because of dextran, the graphitic plane present in the surface of CQD leaves oxygen free Lewis site and increase the acidic functional groups resulting in positive zeta potential value [58]. In order to improve the stability PVA is added as a binder and the zeta potential of CQD/Dex/PVA is recorded in the Fig. S2c. It is interesting to notice that a single peak of zeta potential is appeared at −7.3 mV with a zeta deviation of 5.17 mV. This unique single peak proves the recovery of stability of CQD/Dex/PVA composite. From physical observation it is observed that the aqueous solution of this composite is a clear solution unlike the turbidity solution of CQD/Dex. Hence, PVA gives a supplementary stability to composite before addition of glucose. Similar kind of observation is also noticed in previous literatures, where PVA is giving stability to organic solar cells [59], chitosan-PVA-silver nanocomposite coating [60], PVA-SrTiO3 films [61], and PVA:ZnO nanocomposite [62]. Again, the zeta potential of CQD/Dex/PVA@Glu is measured as shown in the Fig. S2d, where surface negative charge of zeta potential is obtained at −5.66 mV with a small zeta deviation of 3.47 mV. The sharp peak of zeta potential gives the evidence of good colloidal stability of the prepared material.

The surface morphology of the nanocomposite is studied by using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) with the accelerating voltage of 5 kV and magnification range from 10 μm to 1 μm. Fig. 1a and Fig. 1b depicts the surface morphology of CQD/Dex/PVA at magnification of 10 μm and 1 μm. These resemble a fracture surface involving layered and dent like structure on PVA polymer. The dent observed is attributed to the doping of CQD and dextran on PVA depending on few environmental conditions. The well attachment of thick distorted spherical dextran molecules (red colour) and petal like tiny CQD particles (blue colour) on PVA surface via interfacial interaction is shown in the Fig. 1c [63,64]. The surface study for CQD/Dex/PVA@Glu is found to be quite interesting as the incorporation of glucose imparts the ceasing of dents on PVA along with presence of agglomerated particles on it (Fig. 1d,e). The successful attachment of perfectly spherical glucose particle embedded by tiny CQDs on PVA polymer surface is confirmed from the Fig. 1f. This proves that the dents are filled by the glucose on PVA surface which confirmed successful synthesis of CQD/Dex/PVA@Glu nanocomposite. The change in morphology due to inclusion of glucose is a motivation for studying the sensing of glucose by as-synthesized material. Details of this is elaborated by fluorescence study. The elemental detection study (EDX) is performed for this nanocomposite and represented in the Fig. 1g-i. The EDX mapping represents the presence of C and O contributed by the agglomerated portion of CQD/Dex/PVA@Glu nanocomposite (Fig. 1g). Fig. 1i gives a quantitative description of elements associated with the nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glucose by CQD/Dex/PVA nanocomposite

ce:section.ce:para: To state the uniqueness of incorporation of CQD on dextran/PVA polymer matrix for glucose detection in comparison to the use of individual dextran or PVA as matrix we did the control experiment. We considered two conditions by excluding PVA and dextran from the first and second composites respectively. The fluorescence emission peaks of CQD, CQD/Dex, and CQD/Dex@Glu are given in the Fig. 2 a. It is observed that when we added glucose to the CQD/Dex composites without adding PVA, the fluorescence intensity of the CQD/Dex@Glu is decreased to 461, but the decrease in fluorescence intensity in this case is less as compared to when PVA is present in the composite. Again, in second case as shown in the Fig. 2b, when dextran is absent in the matrix not only the decrease of fluorescence intensity after adding glucose is reduced, but also the increase in fluorescence intensity of CQD with only addition of PVA without dextran is negligible. Although, in both cases the observation is in accordance with the “switch-OFF” mechanism of the glucose detection however, the extent of quenching in fluorescence intensity in these cases is very less. Due to this individual dextran or PVA with CQD cannot be taken as probe for the sensing of glucose. Again, in order to obtain the clarity in the combined effect of dextran and PVA, the emission spectra of CQD/Dex/PVA and CQD/Dex/PVA@Glu are compared in the Fig. 2c. It is found that the fluorescence intensity of CQD/Dex/PVA nanocomposite is lowered to 447 as compared to CQD, CQD/Dex, and CQD/PVA which may be due to the restriction in movement of dextran chain by entanglement of PVA polymeric chain with dextran backbone. Here, the extra reduction in intensity due to PVA is compensated as PVA is chosen deliberately as binder to acquire better stability of the nanocomposite. Then the fluorescence emission spectra give further reduction in intensity to 426 by the addition of glucose. This result strongly supports the “switch-OFF” mechanism for the sensing of glucose by fluorescence technique. Similar results are appeared in the reported references where the intensity of Au NCs [65] and AgNPs-CdSe QDs [66] decreased because of glucose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity in presence of other saccharides
ce:section.ce:para: The distinguished sensing of target molecule in presence of other interferences in the biological system is extremely important for a biosensor. Therefore, in order to study the selectivity in sensing of glucose with other interfering sugars, the fluorescence emission spectra of glucose are compared with other sugars like fructose, galactose, mannose, arabinose and xylose (Fig. 2d). The concentrations of all sugars including glucose are kept same during the study of selectivity. Significant quenching in fluorescence intensity of CQD/Dex/PVA nanocomposite is observed by the addition of glucose whereas the quenching in case of other sugar molecules is negligible. This observation clearly indicated the high selectivity nature of the as-synthesized CQD/Dex/PVA nanocomposite to glucose over other analogous sugar molecules and encouraged to proceed towards selective glucose detection in real human blood samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stimuli responsive behaviour of CQD/Dex/PVA nanocomposite for the sensing of glucose
ce:section.ce:para: The study of responses of CQD/Dex/PVA nanocomposite to different complex ambient environments including extreme ionic strength and pH conditions is crucial for real field detection. Fluorescence intensities of CQD/Dex/PVA and CQD/Dex/PVA@Glu at different pH values is represented in the Fig. S3. It is noticed that, the fluorescence emission intensities of CQD/Dex/PVA increases up to pH 6 and decreases to pH 8 and then increases to pH 11. Again, the fluorescence emission intensities of CQD/Dex/PVA@Glu decreases gradually till pH 7 and after that in all basic pH conditions the fluorescence intensity remained almost similar. As we know the pH of blood is usually lies between 7.35 and 7.45, so the decrease in intensity in this range is added evidence towards the excellent behaviour of this nanocomposite in blood glucose detection. Hence, the pH responsiveness of as-synthesized material supports the “switch-OFF” detection principle of glucose. Furthermore, to state the effect of ionic strengths on CQD/Dex/PVA nanocomposite for sensing of glucose, the fluorescence intensities of CQD/Dex/PVA and CQD/Dex/PVA@Glu at different concentrations of NaCl from 0.2 to 1 M is given in the Fig. S4. It is found that the fluorescence intensities remained constant with increasing ionic strengths indicating the absence of any highly ionized functional group on the surface of nanocomposites. Therefore, the excellent stability of the as-synthesized nanocomposite under different stimuli conditions for detection of glucose is evidenced.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of concentration variation of CQD
ce:section.ce:para: A series of composites of CQD/Dex are prepared by taking different concentrations of CQD to demonstrate the effect of concentration of CQD on the fluorescence intensity. (Fig. S5) CQD is taken in four different ratios to dextran i.e., Dex:CQD at 1:0.25, 1:0.5, 1:0.75 and 1:1. It is noticed that with increase in concentration of CQD in CQD/Dex composite the fluorescence intensity increases and a remarkable increased intensity is observed at 1:1 Dex:CQD concentration. So, 1:1 Dex:CQD composition is considered for subsequent experiments.

The calibration curve shown in the Fig. 3 a is obtained by taking fluorescence emission spectra at different concentration of glucose starting from 0 to 100 mg/mL. The fluorescence intensity of CQD/Dex/PVA nanocomposite decreases monotonically with increase in concentration of glucose from 0 to 100 mg/mL as shown in the Fig. S6. The corresponding calibration plot showing the linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and glucose concentration is given in inset of Fig. 3a. The regression equation is y = -38.8x + 346.22 with a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.986. The linear range of the method is found to be 0.27 mM to 1.72 mM and the limit of detection (LOD) for glucose by the as-synthesized CQD/Dex/PVA nanocomposite is calculated to be 61.1 μM by using the formula; LOD = 3σ/m, where σ is the standard deviation and m is the slope of calibration plot given in inset of the Fig. 3a. The calculated LOD obtained from this method is compared with the reported glucose sensing composites and found to be better than the composites are listed in the Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glucose in real human blood serums by CQD/Dex/PVA nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para:
To validate the feasibility of CQD/Dex/PVA nanocomposite towards practical glucose sensing application, the proposed technique is applied for the detection of blood glucose level in real human blood samples. The intensities of fluorescence emission spectra of CQD/Dex/PVA nanocomposite after the addition of blood serums are recorded and is given in the Fig. S7. As obvious, the fluorescence intensities for all twelve blood serums decreased than that of CQD/Dex/PVA nanocomposite. From these recorded fluorescence intensities, the glucose concentration of all twelve human blood serums is measured and is presented in the Fig. 3b. The glucose level of twelve blood samples is found in the range of 4.4–23.9 mM. Considering the accepted blood glucose levels in diabetic, prediabetic and normal people, eight blood specimens are determined to be diabetic, three blood samples are prediabetic and one blood specimen is normal. To demonstrate the reliability, the glucose concentrations of twelve blood serums are measured by commercial glucometer and the values are compared with the concentrations of glucose detected by the proposed fluorescence method as shown in the Table 2
. We found close resemblance between both results obtained from the proposed method and glucometer, with the recovery percentage ranging from 93.9% to 107.8% and relative standard deviation (RSD) from 0.8% to 4.6%. These results clearly evidenced the suitability of the proposed blood glucose sensing method with adequate accuracy and reliability. The details of detection of glucose concentration in all twelve blood samples by the proposed fluorescence technique and its comparison with the measurement by the commercial glucometer are schematically represented as Scheme 2 .

CQD/Dex/PVA nanocomposites are prepared by low cost, green technique where CQD is prepared from natural source. Zeta potential study indicated the formation of highly stable and functionalized CQD. Here, dextran plays a major role for "switch-OFF" mechanism during the detection of glucose where PVA is used as a minor component and acts as a binder for the system. With combined effect of dextran, PVA and CQD a highly sensitive and selective biosensor is designed with better accuracy and stability. The limit of detection is found to be as low as 61.1 μM with a linear range from 0.27 mM to 1.72 mM. Excellent reliability of the method is observed when the glucose content in twelve blood samples measured by this technique is compared with the glucose contents obtained by commercial glucometer. From which closely matchable results and satisfactory recoveries i.e., 93.9% to 107.8% are obtained. From twelve human blood specimens eight are found to be diabetic with three prediabetic and one normal sample. The high reliability and accuracy of this process might promisingly help in diabetes monitoring and diagnosis. Furthermore, the proposed method could also be used for clinical research by providing new insights for pharmaceutical and biological sensing and diagnostics.

Swapnita Patra: Conceptualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Shuvendu Shuvankar Purohit: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Sarat K. Swain: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.
